standaloneHeadlessNotComposition.rationale: Resolved in #452 (A/A): the cross-block coordination is real machinery (event wiring + the page stage), not prose a consumer hand-wires. It renders no DOM of its own — it coordinates the data-table + pagination blocks that do.

reuseApplyPipeline.description: Reuse the data-table's verified `applyPipeline` for filter/sort/group; add only the page stage + event wiring
reuseApplyPipeline.rationale: `applyPipeline`'s page stage is intentionally a no-op ('the Pagination block's job'). The coordinator supplies exactly that missing stage and the event routing, re-implementing none of the verified contract.

patterns: TanStack Table's single getRowModel pipeline (filter -> sort -> group -> paginate)
react.notes: TanStack runs all stages over one row model in order; we split the stages across composable blocks and add a coordinator that sequences them so order stays collection-wide.

name: Pagination
summary: The blocks-layer home for the Collection Operations `page` stage. Renders paged controls inside a `<nav aria-label="pagination">` landmark with `aria-current="page"`, syncs the active page to a shareable self-canonical `?page=n` URL via the History API (Navigation API as enhancement; cross-document View Transitions for zero-JS server-rendered transitions), and wires `advance:auto` to an IntersectionObserver scroll sentinel. Numbered / prev-next / load-more presentations over the orthogonal `pageMode` × `advance` axes; infinite scroll = append + auto, never a peer mode. Ships a consumable `PaginationBehavior` (and a `<page-nav>` element) that mounts the verified renderer, delegates page / load-more clicks, clamps + advances the page state, and emits `pagination-change`; first consumer is the loan-origination pipeline (#317).

paginationNavLandmark.pattern: Pagination controls wrapped in a `<nav aria-label="pagination">` landmark; the active page link marked `aria-current="page"`
paginationNavLandmark.adoption: PaginationBehavior renders the nav landmark and projects the active page as aria-current='page'; each landmark gets a unique label when more than one exists

ariaCurrent.pattern: aria-current='page' marks the active page within the pagination nav
ariaCurrent.adoption: Set on the active page link; the companion sortDirection->aria-sort projection on table headers is deferred to the grid block

historyApi.pattern: pushState/popstate/scrollRestoration sync ?page=n to a shareable, back-button-restorable URL
historyApi.adoption: The native-first baseline for urlSync='query-param'; the active page is a self-canonical ?page=n and restores on back/forward

navigationApi.pattern: A single navigate event intercepts same-origin same-document navigations
navigationApi.adoption: Progressive enhancement over the History API baseline — used when available, never required

crossDocumentViewTransitions.pattern: @view-transition { navigation: auto; } animates full-page navigation between server-rendered ?page=n pages with zero JS
crossDocumentViewTransitions.adoption: The MPA/server-rendered branch opts in via the CSS rule on both pages; no script required

intersectionObserver.pattern: A scroll sentinel observed by IntersectionObserver requests the next slice at the scroll boundary
intersectionObserver.adoption: Wires advance:auto (append + auto = infinite scroll); rendering delegates to Windowed Collection, lifecycle to Loader

selfCanonicalUrls.pattern: Each page is its own unique self-referencing canonical ?page=n; rel=next/prev is deprecated (2019) and never emitted as primary machinery
selfCanonicalUrls.adoption: Server-rendered <a href> page links are the indexable default; load-more/auto content provides a crawlable paginated path alongside the interactive UI

dedicatedBlockOverGrid.description: A dedicated pagination block realizing the `page` dimension family, rather than folding it into a broad grid/data-table block
dedicatedBlockOverGrid.rationale: Every concrete renderable in the gap is pagination (nav landmark, aria-current, page-size selector, range label, scroll sentinel). The filter/sort/group rendering home (and the sortDirection->aria-sort projection, a table-header concern) is a separate, larger block tracked independently. Keeps this contract bounded and demonstrable against the verified WAI-ARIA APG pattern.

pageModeXAdvance.description: Two orthogonal axes — pageMode (paged | append) x advance (manual | auto) — not one coupled `mode` list
pageModeXAdvance.rationale: Infinite scroll is a derived opt-in (append + auto), not a peer mode. paged + auto is undefined (you do not auto-replace a slice on scroll). Matches the Collection Operations intent vocabulary verbatim.

nativeFirstUrlState.description: History API is the baseline for urlSync; Navigation API and cross-document View Transitions are enhancements
nativeFirstUrlState.rationale: Native-first: the History API has been Baseline since 2020 and covers ?page=n + back/forward + scroll restoration with no library. The Navigation API (Baseline 2026) and zero-JS View Transitions layer on where supported.

seoLoadBearing.description: urlSync is load-bearing for append modes, not optional polish
seoLoadBearing.rationale: Googlebot does not fire user-action JS, so load-more/auto content is not reliably crawlable; append modes are only advisable with query-param URL sync on, else the footer and back button break. Server-rendered self-canonical ?page=n links are the SEO-safe default; rel=next/prev is dead.

rangeLabelRequiresTotal.description: rangeLabel ('showing 21-40 of 500') and jump-to-page compose only over an offset/page resource protocol
rangeLabelRequiresTotal.rationale: A cursor protocol has no total, so it structurally forces prev-next or load-more and forces rangeLabel off. This cross-layer constraint is inherited from the Custom Pagination resource, not re-modeled here.
rangeLabelRequiresTotal.openQuestion: Recommended page-size options and defaults, and how the range label degrades with no total, are unverified — owned by backlog #060.

contractsOnly.description: This block defines the contract (nav structure, observable events, URL-state and advance strategies); concrete strategy implementations live in Frontier UI
contractsOnly.rationale: Web Everything is the standard. The block names the registry/strategy seams; competing implementations (a custom URL-state provider, an alternate advance trigger) register against them without changing the contract.

focusAfterPageChange.description: Focus and screen-reader announcement after a page change are intentionally unresolved here
focusAfterPageChange.rationale: The APG landmark + aria-current contract is verified; post-change focus/announcement is an open question tracked in backlog #059 and must not be guessed.

react.notes: TanStack models the page state; React Aria supplies the a11y wiring. We compose both into one declarative block over the intent.

vue.notes: Component-level numbered pagination with aria-current; no built-in cursor/offset protocol coupling.

github.notes: Server-rendered <a href> numbered links inside a nav landmark — closest to our SEO-safe default.

adobe.notes: Prior art for the result-range label and page-size selector affordances (defaults pending #060).

traits.name: withRangeLabel
traits.description: Surfaces the slice as a 'showing 21-40 of 500' result-range label. Requires a known total -> offset/page protocol only.

traits.name: withPageSizeSelect
traits.description: Adds a page-size selector (e.g. 10 / 25 / 50 / 100). Recommended options and persistence pending #060.

traits.name: withAutoAdvance
traits.description: Turns append into infinite scroll by fetching the next slice at an IntersectionObserver scroll sentinel (advance:auto).

traits.name: withUrlSync
traits.description: Reflects the active page in a shareable, back-button-restorable ?page=n URL via the History API.

name: Selection
summary: A runtime for the Collection Operations sibling `selection` intent (model / immediacy / variant / grouping) — the part the draft dropdown/droplist blocks compose but no block shipped. `SelectionBehavior` manages which item(s) in a host container are selected, over items matched by a CSS selector: it projects `aria-selected`, keeps a roving `tabindex`, commits selection live on click and Arrow/Home/End/Enter/Space (single keeps at most one, multiple toggles), and emits a `selection-change` event. The `variant:item` list-selection realization; dropdown/droplist will compose it for listbox selection.

name: Lifecycle
summary: Reference runtime for the Web Lifecycle protocol (weblifecycle / #353) — a domain entity's declared status set + guarded, role-scoped transition map. Ships the protocol's seam: a data-defined `LifecycleDefinition` (the only lock; portable across engines), the swappable `CustomLifecycleProvider` contract (`available()` + `transition()`), a native-first `DefaultLifecycleProvider` in-memory reference, and the `CustomLifecycleRegistry` (`window.customLifecycles`). Authorization is delegated to a `GuardResolver` (the Web Guards seam; the default is permissive, a real authz provider is async + server-authoritative) and persistence stays the entity's own concern — so the block owns only the state machine. Every applied transition emits a `LifecycleEvent` ({ entity, from, to, actor, at }), the composition seam for audit-trail (#357). First consumer: the loan-origination exercise app (#317).

name: Status Indicator
summary: Reference render for the Status Indicator intent (#354), the visual member of the Web Lifecycle protocol. `renderStatusIndicator` projects an entity's current lifecycle state onto a semantic status token across the intent's `tone` (neutral|info|progress|positive|caution|critical), `shape` (badge|pill|dot|text), and `affordance` (display-only|actionable) dimensions. It owns the display, not the rules: tone maps to a theme token (never a bespoke hex), the label text carries the state (never colour-only), and `role="status"` marks the standing indicator while a transition announcement composes the Live Region Status intent. Ships an element form and a string form (`statusIndicatorHTML`) for table/innerHTML contexts.

name: Audit
summary: Reference runtime for the Web Audit protocol (webaudit / #357) — a domain entity's immutable, actor-attributed history. Ships the protocol's seam: the normalized `AuditEvent` ({ target, action, actor, at, before?, after?, correlationId? }, before/after reusing the Web States `ChangeRecord` shape), the swappable `CustomAuditProvider` contract (`append()` / `queryByEntity()` / optional `subscribe()`), a native-first append-only `DefaultAuditProvider` (it freezes each event — never mutates or deletes), and the `CustomAuditRegistry` (`window.customAudit`). The `auditLifecycle()` helper is the headline composition: it subscribes a Web Lifecycle provider so every transition becomes one `AuditEvent` automatically. First consumer: the loan-origination exercise app (#317).

name: Audit Timeline
summary: Reference render for the Audit Timeline intent, the visual member of the Web Audit protocol. `renderAuditTimeline` renders an entity's `AuditEvent[]` as a chronological, filterable feed across the intent's `density` (compact|comfortable), `grouping` (flat|by-day|by-actor), and `detail` (summary|expanded) dimensions, with `role="list"` and `<time datetime>` semantics. It owns the display, not the record — append-only semantics, actor attribution, and before/after belong to the protocol. Ships an element form and a string form (`auditTimelineHTML`).

name: Decision Trace
summary: Reference render for the Decision Trace intent, the visual member of the Web Decisions protocol (decision-record / #355). `renderDecisionTrace(record)` turns a normalized `DecisionRecord` — why a rules/criteria evaluation reached its outcome — into a criteria trace table (input/operator/threshold/outcome per criterion) plus the aggregated outcome and reason codes, across the intent's `layout` (table|list), `emphasis` (all-criteria|deciding), and `disclosure` dimensions. Schema-only standard: the render owns the explanation UI, not the rules. Composes the status-indicator block for the outcome and per-criterion tokens. Ships an element form and a string form (`decisionTraceHTML`).

name: Master-Detail
summary: Reference coordinator for the master-detail intent (#356) — pairs a collection (master list) with a coupled detail region. `MasterDetailBehavior(masterEl, { itemSelector, detailEl, keyOf, renderDetail, focusFlow?, emptyState? })` COMPOSES the shipping selection block (it does not reimplement selection): on select it renders the detail for the item's key (async-aware, toggling an `md-loading` Loader seam), wires the detail as a labelled `role="region"`, applies the focus flow (retain|advance), and handles the empty state (placeholder|collapse), emitting `detail-change`. URL deep-linking is left to the consumer's router (compose, don't duplicate). First consumer: the loan-origination exercise app (#317).

name: Tree Select
summary: The hierarchical member of the droplist family (per the #064 ruling). `TreeSelectBehavior` realizes the `hierarchy` intent: a `role="tree"` of `role="treeitem"` / `role="group"` with `aria-level` / `aria-setsize` / `aria-posinset`, Right/Left expand-then-descend / collapse-then-ascend traversal, Up/Down/Home/End movement, and node selection (`aria-checked`, single|multiple) with an optional `cascade` (a partially-checked parent reads `mixed`). Builds the tree from a declarative `TreeNode[]` and emits `tree-change`. Native-first; degrades conceptually to a grouped `<select>`. First consumer: the auto-insurance coverage builder (#318).

name: Data Table
summary: The blocks-layer home for the Collection Operations `filter`, `sort`, and `group` stages (the `page` stage is the companion Pagination block — compose, never merge). Renders a native `<table>` whose sortable column headers project `sortDirection` onto the WAI-ARIA APG `aria-sort` attribute (ascending/descending, sortable-but-unsorted = none) via button-wrapped `<th scope="col">`, orders rows with `Intl.Collator` (sensitivity/numeric/caseFirst/ignorePunctuation) and SQL-style empty placement, narrows them by `filterMatch` (all/any), and partitions them into `<tbody>` groups with `groupSummary` aggregates named after the SQL functions (count/sum/average/minimum/maximum). The pipeline order is data (default filter → sort → group → page). Ships a consumable `DataTableBehavior` (and a `<data-table>` custom element) that mounts the verified renderer, wires click-to-sort + a polite live-region announcement, and emits `data-table-change`; its first enterprise consumer is the loan-origination exercise app (#317).

apgSortableTable.pattern: Tabular data in a native `<table>` with `<th scope="col">` headers; sortable headers wrap their label in a `<button>` and the active column carries `aria-sort`
apgSortableTable.adoption: DataTableBehavior renders the table and projects the primary sort key onto `aria-sort` on its `<th>`; sortable-but-unsorted headers are `aria-sort="none"`, non-sortable headers carry none

ariaSort.pattern: aria-sort = ascending | descending | none | other, on one header at a time
ariaSort.adoption: The intent's `sortDirection` projects verbatim onto `aria-sort`; only the primary sort key's header is marked (APG: one sorted header at a time), even under a multi-key sort

intlCollator.adoption: The reference comparator is `Intl.Collator`; the intent's text-comparison dimensions adopt its option names verbatim, so `numeric:true` gives natural order (img2 before img10) with no bespoke comparator

arrayToSorted.adoption: Stable sort makes multi-key tiebreaking and `sortKeys:single` (which preserves prior order) well-defined without extra bookkeeping

rowgroupScope.pattern: A `<th scope="rowgroup">` associates a header with all cells in its row group
rowgroupScope.adoption: Each group renders as its own `<tbody>` led by a `scope="rowgroup"` summary row carrying the group key + its aggregate, so assistive tech announces the grouping

sortableTableOverGrid.description: Realized as the APG Sortable Table recipe (`<table>` + button-wrapped `<th scope="col">` + `aria-sort`), not the interactive `role="grid"` pattern
sortableTableOverGrid.rationale: filter/sort/group is a read-and-reorder concern; the verified-today accessible pattern is the APG Sortable Table. `role="grid"` layers cell-level keyboard navigation — a separate interactivity axis that composes later, not a prerequisite for the data operations.

intlCollatorComparator.description: `Intl.Collator` is the reference comparator; the intent's comparison dimensions are its option names verbatim
intlCollatorComparator.rationale: Native-first: the platform already standardizes locale-aware comparison. The block never invents a comparator vocabulary — `numericOrdering`/`textSensitivity`/`caseFirst`/`ignorePunctuation` are passed straight through. A different comparator strategy (numeric-only, decorate-sort-undecorate, server-delegated ORDER BY) is a Frontier UI / app concern, catalogued in the Technical Configurator's Sorting Strategy domain.

emptyPlacementDirectionIndependent.description: `emptyPlacement` (first/last) is applied independently of sort direction, mirroring SQL NULLS FIRST/LAST
emptyPlacementDirectionIndependent.rationale: The web platform has no native term for null ordering, so the intent borrows SQL's. Empties do not flip with ascending/descending — `NULLS LAST` stays last in both, which matches every SQL engine and user expectation.

ariaSortOneHeader.description: Only the primary sort key's header is marked aria-sort != none, even under a multi-key sort
ariaSortOneHeader.rationale: WAI-ARIA defines aria-sort as one-header-at-a-time. Multi-key sorting still orders rows by the full key list, but the projected state names the primary column; surfacing secondary keys is a presentational affordance, not an aria-sort value.

sortHeaderProjectionSeam.description: The sortDirection → aria-sort header projection is exposed as a named `sort:header` behavior seam, realized inside this block
sortHeaderProjectionSeam.rationale: It is the one piece plausibly reusable outside a table (e.g. a sortable card-list header bar). Naming the seam keeps the door open, but extracting a standalone behavior block now would be speculative — defer until a second, non-table consumer actually exists.
sortHeaderProjectionSeam.openQuestion: Promote `sort:header` to a standalone behavior block when a non-table consumer appears; tracked as a follow-up rather than built speculatively.

composeNotMerge.description: Pagination (page stage) and Windowed Collection (virtualization) are composed on the same collection, never merged into this block
composeNotMerge.rationale: This block owns only filter/sort/group — selecting and ordering the set. Pagination owns the page slice + URL/advance; Windowed Collection owns which rows are realized in the DOM. They co-reside on one collection as orthogonal axes; folding any of them in here would entangle independent contracts.

contractsOnly.description: This block defines the contract (table structure, aria-sort projection, comparator/aggregate seams, observable events); concrete strategy implementations live in Frontier UI
contractsOnly.rationale: Web Everything is the standard. The block names the seams; competing implementations (an alternate comparator, server-side execution, a virtualization provider) register against them without changing the contract.

patterns: TanStack Table row-model pipeline (filter → sort → group → paginate)
react.notes: TanStack models the four operations as ordered, optional row-model transforms — the architecture this block's configurable pipeline mirrors; React Aria supplies the aria-sort / scope a11y wiring.

agGrid.notes: Prior art for multi-column sort and per-group aggregate summaries; we name the aggregates after SQL rather than a bespoke set.

vue.notes: Component-level sortable/groupable tables; no shared comparison vocabulary — we anchor to Intl.Collator instead.

traits.name: withSortableHeader
traits.description: Projects the active sort key's direction onto `aria-sort` on a button-wrapped `<th scope="col">` (the `sort:header` seam). Sortable-but-unsorted headers are `aria-sort="none"`.

traits.name: withFilter
traits.description: Narrows the rows by combining call-site predicates per `filterMatch` (all = AND, any = OR).

traits.name: withGrouping
traits.description: Partitions rows into `<tbody>` groups, each led by a `scope="rowgroup"` summary row carrying the `groupSummary` aggregate (count/sum/average/minimum/maximum).

name: Data Grid
summary: The interactive twin of the Data Table block: same tabular data, the *other* WAI-ARIA APG pattern. Renders a native `<table role="grid">` of `role="row"` / `role="columnheader"` / `role="gridcell"` cells with cell-level keyboard navigation — arrow keys move one cell (clamping at the edges), Home/End jump to the row ends, Ctrl+Home/Ctrl+End to the grid corners, PageUp/PageDown by a page — over a roving tabindex (exactly one cell is `tabindex="0"`, every other is `-1`). It is the concrete consumer of the Focus Delegation intent (strategy `roving`, orientation `both`); the column-header row is itself navigable. Where the Data Table is read-and-reorder (`role="table"` + `aria-sort`), the Data Grid is navigate-the-cells (`role="grid"` + roving focus) — APG documents them as separate patterns, so they are parallel blocks, not one upgraded in place.

apgDataGrid.pattern: A `role="grid"` of `role="row"` rows and `role="gridcell"` / `role="columnheader"` cells with arrow-key cell navigation and a roving tabindex
apgDataGrid.adoption: DataGridBehavior renders the native `<table>` with the grid roles and realizes the APG keyboard model verbatim (arrows clamp, Home/End to row ends, Ctrl+Home/End to grid corners, PageUp/Down by a page); the header row is navigable

rovingTabindex.pattern: Exactly one descendant is `tabindex="0"` (the active item) and all others are `tabindex="-1"`; the value moves with focus
rovingTabindex.adoption: The active cell carries `tabindex="0"`, every other cell `-1`; a keystroke recomputes the active cell and the tabindex roves to it, so Tab enters/leaves the grid as one stop while arrows move within

ariaGridIndices.pattern: `aria-rowcount` / `aria-colcount` on the grid and `aria-rowindex` / `aria-colindex` on rows and cells declare the full extent and a cell's position
ariaGridIndices.adoption: The grid declares `aria-rowcount` / `aria-colcount` (header row included) and each row/cell carries its 1-based `aria-rowindex` / `aria-colindex`, so the position is announced even when not every row is realized in the DOM

parallelBlockNotUpgrade.description: The Data Grid is its own block alongside the Data Table, not a sortable table "upgraded" with cell navigation
parallelBlockNotUpgrade.rationale: The read-only Sortable Table (`role="table"`, click-to-sort, `aria-sort`) and the interactive Data Grid (`role="grid"`, arrow-key cell focus) are two distinct APG patterns with different interaction models and a11y contracts — APG documents them separately. Folding grid navigation into the Data Table would conflate them. They compose onto the same collection when both reordering and cell traversal are wanted (decision carried from backlog #115).

composesFocusDelegation.description: The grid is a concrete consumer of the Focus Delegation intent (strategy `roving`, orientation `both`), not a bespoke focus model
composesFocusDelegation.rationale: Focus Delegation already standardizes roving tabindex vs. virtual focus, arrow-key movement, and the Tab-between / arrows-within invariant. The grid realizes the `roving` strategy over two axes rather than reinventing focus management; the movement engine is the verifiable seam.

headerRowNavigable.description: The column-header row is part of the navigable grid (arrow-up reaches the headers); positions range over `1 (header) + dataRows` rows
headerRowNavigable.rationale: The APG Data Grid examples include the header row in arrow navigation so a keyboard user can read column labels in place. Modelling row 0 as the header keeps Ctrl+Home a true grid origin and avoids a special-case off-by-one between the visual table and the position model.

clampNotWrap.description: Movement clamps at the grid edges; it does not wrap (last → first)
clampNotWrap.rationale: APG lists wrapping as an opt-in, not the default. Clamping matches the unsurprising default and a spreadsheet's behavior; a wrapping option is a Focus Delegation `wrap` concern a concrete implementation can layer on.

contractsOnly.description: This block defines the contract (grid roles, the roving-tabindex invariant, the keyboard movement model, the audit); concrete strategies live in Frontier UI
contractsOnly.rationale: Web Everything is the standard. The block names the seams (the movement engine, the active-cell projection); competing implementations (virtualized rows, editable cells, server paging, two-dimensional selection) register against them without changing the contract.

patterns: React Aria useGridList / useGrid (roving focus + grid keyboard model)
AG Grid cell navigation (arrow keys, Ctrl+Home/End, Page keys)
react.notes: React Aria supplies the grid/roving-focus a11y wiring this block names as the Focus Delegation seam; AG Grid is prior art for the exact key bindings (arrows, Home/End, Ctrl corners, Page) we adopt from APG.

vue.notes: Component-level grid keyboard navigation; no shared focus vocabulary — we anchor to the APG roving-tabindex model and the Focus Delegation intent instead.

traits.name: withCellNavigation
traits.description: Realizes the APG Data Grid keyboard model over a roving tabindex — arrows (clamped), Home/End, Ctrl+Home/End, PageUp/Down — moving the single `tabindex="0"` cell with DOM focus.

traits.name: withGridRoles
traits.description: Projects the tabular data onto the `role="grid"` / `role="row"` / `role="columnheader"` / `role="gridcell"` structure with `aria-rowcount` / `aria-colcount` and per-cell `aria-rowindex` / `aria-colindex`.

name: Reorderable List
summary: A list whose item order is user-mutable by pointer drag and by keyboard, with live-region announcement and a pluggable commit strategy. Relocates items with atomic Element.moveBefore() so an item keeps its focus/animation/connection state across a move. Manual order — not Collection Operations' computed sort.

pointerEvents.adoption: The default drag path — unifies mouse/touch/pen and captures the pointer for the duration of a drag. Preferred over the HTML5 Drag and Drop API for in-list reorder (better touch + a11y).

elementMoveBefore.adoption: Relocate an item without disconnecting it, preserving focus, animations, iframes, and custom-element connection. Wired declaratively via the Component block's preserve-on-move attribute; falls back to insertBefore where unsupported.

ariaApgDrag.adoption: Roving tabindex over items + grab/move/drop keys; announce moves via a polite live region. The deprecated aria-grabbed/aria-dropeffect are NOT used.

htmlDragAndDrop.pattern: HTML Drag and Drop API (draggable, dragstart/dragover/drop)
htmlDragAndDrop.adoption: Reserved for cross-window and OS file transfer (a data-transfer concern, see backlog #007); NOT the in-list reorder default.

reorder-start.description: Fired when an item is grabbed (pointer down on the grab affordance, or keyboard grab key)

reorder-move.description: Fired as the grabbed item passes a new candidate position during a drag

reorder-commit.description: Fired when a move is committed (dropped). Cancelable — preventDefault to reject the new order (e.g. server validation); the commit strategy listens here to persist

reorder-cancel.description: Fired when a grab is abandoned (Esc, or pointer released outside a valid target); order is unchanged

keyboardParityMandatory.description: A conforming reorderable list provides a keyboard path (roving tabindex + grab/move/drop keys + live-region announcement) equivalent to the pointer path.
keyboardParityMandatory.rationale: WAI-ARIA APG requires a keyboard alternative for drag interactions; the deprecated aria-grabbed/aria-dropeffect are unfit. Pointer-only reorder is non-conforming, not 'partially done'.

pointerNotHtml5Dnd.description: The default drag path uses Pointer Events, not the HTML5 Drag and Drop API.
pointerNotHtml5Dnd.rationale: Pointer Events unify mouse/touch/pen and give clean capture; HTML5 DnD is clunky and weak for touch/a11y. HTML5 DnD is reserved for OS file / cross-window transfer (data-transfer, backlog #007).

atomicMove.description: Item relocation uses Element.moveBefore() (the Component block's preserve-on-move), not remove+insert.
atomicMove.rationale: Reordered cards are often interactive; an atomic move preserves focus, in-flight animations, iframes, and custom-element connection state that a disconnect/reconnect would reset.

manualVsComputedOrder.description: Reorder produces a manual order, mutually exclusive with the Collection Operations sort dimension.
manualVsComputedOrder.rationale: A list is hand-ordered OR comparator-sorted, never both at once; the two intents are distinct answers to 'what determines order' and cross-reference rather than merge.

commitStrategyProvider.description: Where the new order is written is a provider behind the cancelable reorder-commit event, defaulting to ephemeral.
commitStrategyProvider.rationale: Persistence varies (none / localStorage / write-back to source); keep it a strategy the host supplies rather than baking one backend into the block. Start as an attribute; promote to a CustomReorderCommitRegistry only if multiple backends emerge.

traits.name: withDragHandle
traits.description: Restrict the grab affordance to a scoped handle within each item, leaving the rest interactive

traits.name: withPointerReorder
traits.description: Drag-to-reorder via Pointer Events with pointer capture (mouse, touch, pen)

traits.name: withKeyboardReorder
traits.description: Roving-tabindex keyboard path: grab, move with arrows, drop or cancel

traits.name: withLiveAnnounce
traits.description: Announce each move and the final commit via a polite live region (replaces deprecated aria-grabbed)

traits.name: withCommitStrategy
traits.description: Persist the new order (localStorage or write-back) behind the cancelable reorder-commit event

traits.name: withCrossListReorder
traits.description: Move items between sibling lists that share a group key

name: Background Task Surface
summary: The persistent app-shell surface that hosts long-running work after it goes async — the receiving end of a Loader's escalation:async handoff. Renders one entry per registered task with per-task progress (the Loader's own state machine, hosted off-view), per-task retry delegating to Reliability, and arms the navigation-away / before-unload guard while any task is in flight. Distinct from the Feedback Intent (transient toasts) and the Loader Intent (in-view pending state that dies with its view).

customEvent.pattern: Bubbling CustomEvent as the registration handoff
customEvent.adoption: A Loader at escalation:async dispatches a bubbling background-task-register event carrying its loader-state handle; the surface listens and adopts it. A one-shot, fire-and-handoff registration that decouples the surface from the (possibly torn-down) originating element — chosen over an injector/DI registry because the task must outlive its originating view.

beforeunload.pattern: beforeunload prompt for cross-document leave / close
beforeunload.adoption: While navigationGuard:warn and any task is active, the surface arms beforeunload so closing the tab or a full reload prompts the user. Cleared automatically when the last in-flight task resolves. This guard is why the route-only baseline is safe: a hard reload that would lose in-flight work is gated, not silently dropped.

navigationApi.pattern: Navigation API navigate event intercept for same-document route-leave
navigationApi.adoption: The SPA route-leave confirm half of navigationGuard:warn intercepts the Navigation API's navigate event (preventing/confirming same-document navigations) — the same-document counterpart to beforeunload. The two primitives present as one guard contract (harvested as the navigation-guard intent, #129).

backgroundFetch.pattern: Background Fetch API + service worker for reload-durable work
backgroundFetch.adoption: The durability:reload tier — work that genuinely survives a full reload or tab close — is delegated here, NOT baked into the baseline. The intent borrows Background Fetch's vocabulary 'without requiring a service worker', so this surface stays usable as a route-only in-memory baseline and treats reload-durability as an opt-in adapter (see backlog spin-off).

ariaStatusRegion.pattern: Live status region for off-view progress announcement
ariaStatusRegion.adoption: Because the surface is off the user's current view, task completion and failure are announced via a polite live region (role=status) so a screen-reader user learns a backgrounded task finished without watching the rail. Reuses the Live Region Status paradigm rather than redefining it.

background-task-register.description: The handoff IN. A Loader (or any producer) dispatches this when work goes async; the nearest ancestor surface adopts the carried task handle and begins rendering its entry. This is the block's registration mechanism.

detail.label: string
detail.loaderState: object (the Loader state-machine handle, subscribed to off-view)

background-task-state-change.description: Re-emitted by the surface as a task entry moves through the intent lifecycle. Surfaces the Background Task Intent's registered → active → success → error → dismissed states as a single observable event.

background-task-retry.description: Fired when the user activates retry on a failed entry. The surface only exposes the affordance; recovery policy is delegated to the Reliability Intent. Cancelable so a host can intercept and run its own recovery.

background-task-dismiss.description: Fired when an entry is removed — by the user, or by auto-clear for a transient entry that resolved successfully. Cancelable to allow a host to keep a sticky entry.

registrationEventBus.description: Tasks are registered via a bubbling background-task-register CustomEvent, NOT an injector/DI registry.
registrationEventBus.rationale: Registration must outlive the originating view, and it is a one-shot handoff rather than an ongoing dependency. An event decouples the surface from the (possibly already torn-down) originating element; the event carries the Loader's existing state machine, so the surface subscribes to a handle it did not create — the intent's 'same machine, hosted off-view'. This is a fixed mechanic, not a configurable choice: two registration protocols would mean no protocol.

nearestAncestorResolution.description: A register event routes to the nearest ancestor surface; an app places one <background-tasks> in its shell, giving a single global instance by default while allowing a composable per-shell surface.
nearestAncestorResolution.rationale: Falls straight out of the bubbling-event choice — exactly like event bubbling finds the first listener. The platform analog (Background Fetch UI, a download manager) is one global surface, but an embedded sub-app can drop in its own surface with no extra config. Also a fixed mechanic, not an offered choice.

durabilityRouteBaseline.description: The baseline surface is route-only (survives SPA route changes, in-memory); reload-durability is an opt-in durability:reload dimension delegated to the Background Fetch / service-worker adapter.
durabilityRouteBaseline.rationale: Native-first: the platform's durable answer (Background Fetch) is a documented enhancement, not the baseline dependency — the intent explicitly borrows its vocabulary 'without requiring a service worker'. The navigationGuard:warn prompt covers the gap a hard reload would otherwise leave. Unlike the two mechanics above, this IS a legitimate per-product choice, so it is exposed as a dimension.

fixedMechanicsVsConfigurableDimensions.description: Internal mechanics (registration via event bus, nearest-ancestor resolution) are fixed; user-facing axes are exposed as dimensions: aggregation (single/batch), persistence (transient/sticky), navigationGuard (none/warn), and the block-added durability (route/reload).
fixedMechanicsVsConfigurableDimensions.rationale: A fork becomes a configurable axis only when both branches are legitimate end-states for different products; it stays a fixed decision when one branch is simply the correct way to build the contract and variants would fragment interop. The three intent dimensions plus durability meet that bar; the two registration mechanics do not.

reusesLoaderStateMachine.description: Per-task progress is the Loader Intent's determinate/indeterminate indicator, version/generation token, and lifecycle — the same state machine, hosted off-view — not a reimplementation.
reusesLoaderStateMachine.rationale: A backgrounded task is a Loader that crossed the escalation:async threshold; reusing its machine keeps progress semantics identical on- and off-view and means the handoff transfers a live handle rather than snapshotting state.

distinctFromFeedbackAndLoader.description: This surface is neither the Feedback Intent (transient fire-and-forget toasts) nor the Loader Intent (in-view pending state that dies with its view); it owns ongoing off-view task state and may emit a Feedback toast downstream on completion.
distinctFromFeedbackAndLoader.rationale: Keeps the three surfaces from collapsing: Feedback does not track ongoing state, the Loader does not outlive its view, and only this surface aggregates concurrent tasks with per-task retry.

traits.name: withBatchAggregation
traits.description: Present multiple concurrent tasks, each with its own progress and retry, where one task's failure does not abort the others (default is single — one entry at a time)

traits.name: withStickyEntries
traits.description: Keep a resolved entry until explicitly dismissed instead of auto-clearing on success (error entries are sticky regardless, so a failure that happened off-view is still reviewable)

traits.name: withNavigationGuard
traits.description: While any task is active, arm the beforeunload prompt (cross-document close/reload) and the Navigation API route-leave confirm (same-document); cleared when the last in-flight task resolves

traits.name: withCompletionToast
traits.description: Emit a transient Feedback Intent toast when a task completes, for the case where the user is on another view and the rail entry auto-clears

traits.name: withPerTaskRetry
traits.description: Expose a retry affordance on a failed entry that delegates recovery to the Reliability Intent (the surface owns the affordance, not the policy)

traits.name: withReloadDurability
traits.description: Opt into reload-durable work via the Background Fetch / service-worker adapter so an in-flight task survives a full page reload or tab close (default is route-only, in-memory)

name: Tooltip
summary: A small, transient, non-interactive surface that describes its trigger on hover or keyboard focus and is wired as the trigger's accessible description (aria-describedby, never aria-label). Native-first: it extends the built-in title attribute and the Popover API's popover="hint" where supported, falling back to an ARIA-described custom bubble only when richer content or collision-aware positioning is needed. It composes the Anchor Intent (placement + flip/shift at the viewport edge), the Surface Intent (floating elevation/material of the bubble), and the Typography Intent (the truncation-recovery case that reads a clipped label). Deliberately distinct from a toggletip (click-revealed, live-region-announced) and from a non-modal popover with interactive content.
designDecisions: Describes, never names — aria-describedby, not aria-label. A tooltip supplements an already-named control with extra description; it is not the control's name. Wiring it as aria-label would replace the accessible name (and silently lose it when the tooltip is hidden), so the trigger keeps its own name and the tooltip is referenced via aria-describedby. This also means a tooltip is never the sole carrier of essential information — content that must be perceivable belongs in the always-rendered DOM, not in a hover-only surface.

Native-first ladder: title → popover=hint → ARIA bubble. The built-in title attribute is the zero-cost baseline (free hover text, but no styling, no touch, no focus parity, and an uncontrollable UA delay), so the block extends it rather than forbidding it. The Popover API's popover="hint" is the next rung — a real top-layer element with light-dismiss, styleable, that auto-closes when another hint opens — and is the default substrate where Baseline-supported. A fully custom ARIA-described bubble is the top rung, used only when content is richer than text or when Anchor-Intent collision handling (flip/shift/resize) is required. Third-party positioning (Floating UI) is an opt-in adapter behind the Anchor Intent, never the baseline.

Hover and keyboard-focus parity, with WCAG 1.4.13 obligations. The tooltip shows on pointer hover AND on keyboard focus of the trigger (parity is non-negotiable — a mouse-only tooltip is inaccessible), with a short show delay (~500ms hover, immediate on focus) and a hide delay. Per WCAG 1.4.13 (Content on Hover or Focus) the surface must be hoverable (the pointer can move onto the tooltip without it vanishing — a pointer-leave grace period bridges the gap), persistent (it stays until focus/hover leaves or Escape), and dismissible (Escape hides it without moving focus). There is no active-item model and no selection.

Tooltip ≠ toggletip ≠ popover — three distinct contracts. A tooltip is hover/focus-revealed, non-interactive, and announced as a description. A toggletip is click/tap-revealed, can be re-summoned, and is announced via a live region (because there is no persistent describing relationship) — it is a separate concern, cross-referenced, not a flag on this block. A non-modal popover holds interactive content the user can Tab into; the moment content is interactive it is no longer a tooltip (focus can't live in a tooltip). Keeping these separate stops the common anti-pattern of putting buttons or links inside a tooltip.

name: Slider
summary: A control for selecting a numeric value — or a min–max range — along a track. It realizes the Selection Intent's range variant (SelectionVariant = 'boolean' | 'item' | 'range'), which no block delivered before. Native-first by construction: a single-thumb slider IS <input type="range">, so the block's job is only the enhancements the native control cannot express alone — a dual-thumb (from–to) range, rich ticks and labelled marks, and locale-aware value formatting (units, number format) surfaced through aria-valuetext. It composes the Selection Intent (variant=range, constraints.min/max), the Input Intent (pointer drag vs keyboard vs touch modality), and the Locale / Translation intents (value formatting). Native stays the baseline; a custom track/thumb is used only when the native element is ruled out.
designDecisions: Single-thumb slider IS native <input type="range"> — enhance, don't replace. A one-thumb slider is already a complete, accessible, form-associated platform control: keyboard arrows, step, focusability, and form submission all come free. The block defaults to the native element and only swaps to a custom track/thumb when a native gap is hit (dual-thumb, vertical orientation that the platform doesn't reliably expose, or rich tick rendering). This keeps the common case zero-JS and form-native, and confines custom code to the cases that genuinely need it.

Dual-thumb range is two bound values, not one element. The platform has no single dual-thumb range input, so a from–to range is modelled as two coordinated thumbs over one track, each a value with its own aria-valuenow/min/max, kept ordered (the low thumb can't cross the high thumb) and reported as a pair. This is the concrete realization of the Selection Intent's range variant — constraints.min and constraints.max become the two thumb bounds — rather than a new paradigm invented here.

Value reporting is formatted, not raw — aria-valuetext over the Locale Intent. A slider's accessible value is whatever the user should hear ('$1,200', '40%', '3.5 km'), not the bare number, so formatted output flows through the Locale / Translation intents and is published via aria-valuetext while aria-valuenow keeps the raw number for AT that computes percentages. RTL flips the track direction so 'increase' still maps to the start-to-end reading order.

Slider is the continuous-along-a-track member, distinct from a stepper. Stepping by keyboard arrows/PageUp-Down and snapping to ticks are in scope, but a slider is fundamentally for choosing a value by position on a track; precise numeric entry is a future numeric/stepper input's job. Naming that boundary now keeps the slider from absorbing spinner/number-field concerns and keeps the two as separate Selection-Intent members.

name: Segmented Control
summary: A single-select choice rendered as a row of adjacent buttons — the iOS/macOS segmented control, or 'pill' toggle group. It is the Selection Intent's single model in a horizontal, always-visible variant (compare radio group / select / switch): every option is visible at once and exactly one is pressed. It composes the Selection Intent (model=single, live immediacy — selecting commits immediately), Focus Delegation (roving tabindex, horizontal orientation with arrow-key movement), and the Typography / Density intents for sizing. Its options are authored as compound children (<segment> elements inside the control) that map to the value model — an instance of the cross-cutting compound-child paradigm (see #176's spin-off) shared with Tabs and the native <select>/<option>.
designDecisions: It is Selection single, always-visible and horizontal — not a new selection model. A segmented control is the same single-select contract as a radio group or a <select>; what differs is presentation (all options visible at once, laid out as adjacent pressed buttons) and immediacy (live — choosing commits immediately, no separate confirm). So it sets selection="model=single" with live immediacy rather than introducing any new dimension, which keeps it swappable with the other single-select members under one ambient selectionIntent.

Roving tabindex, horizontal — one tab stop, arrows move within. Per the WAI-ARIA radio/tablist pattern the control is a single tab stop: Tab enters at the pressed segment, Left/Right (roving) move and (because immediacy is live) select as they move, Home/End jump to ends. This is delegated wholesale to the Focus Delegation Intent (strategy=roving;orientation=horizontal) rather than re-implemented, so keyboard behavior stays identical to every other roving widget.

Options are authored as compound children, not an options prop. The author writes <segment> children inside the control and the block maps each to a value-model entry; this is the same shape as <tab> inside Tabs and <option> inside <select>. The segmented control is the smallest clean instance of that recurring pattern, so it adopts it directly. Whether that 'compound child → option' pattern should be promoted to its own protocol/semantics contract (vs. remaining documentation of Selection's grouping) is a genuine cross-cutting fork, deliberately NOT settled inside this block — it is carried as a separate decision item. Recommended default there: document it as a shared paradigm (a protocol/semantics term cross-referenced by Selection + Tabs), not a new UX intent, since it is an authoring-and-lowering convention with no UX dimensions of its own.

Distinct from a multi-select button group and from Tabs. Each option pressed-or-not but exactly one pressed (aria-pressed / a rad/tablist-style roles) separates it from a toolbar of independent toggle buttons (multi-select, no roving-select). And although it shares the compound-child shape with Tabs, Tabs control which panel is shown (disclosure of associated regions) whereas a segmented control sets a value; keeping them separate blocks stops the value-vs-panel concerns from merging.

name: Menu
summary: Composition manifest for the action-invocation menu family — the ARIA menu / menubar / menuitem surface for invoking COMMANDS, not selecting values. One block with a shape: menubar | menu | context dimension (orientation derived); roving focus as a fixed mechanic (overriding the droplist family virtual default); every item composes the command intent for invocation; checkable items reuse Selection under a checkable trait; submenus compose Disclosure + Anchor (not the tree-select hierarchy intent); separator and group are owned directly. Distinct from the droplist family, which are listbox/combobox value selectors. Ratified in #173.
designDecisions: Invocation, not value (the menu's thesis). A menu item INVOKES an action — a command — which is a different paradigm from committing a value, and the whole reason the menu warrants its own block separate from the droplist family (listbox/combobox value-selectors). Every item composes the command intent (#016/#299) for invocation; the Action intent stays orthogonal (visual prominence — primary/destructive), and an item composes both. Overloading Action or Selection for invocation is rejected: it dissolves the menu-vs-select distinction this block exists to draw (#173 Fork A).

Roving focus, fixed — not a dimension. focus-delegation.strategy = roving with selectionFollowsFocus = independent, a FIXED menu mechanic overriding the droplist family's virtual default. APG permits roving OR aria-activedescendant for menus with no menu-specific preference; WE chooses roving by convention (what Radix/Headless/MUI/Fluent all ship). It is not exposed as a configurable axis because there is no legitimate desktop-menu end-state that uses virtual (the only virtual case — a giant virtualized menu — is out of scope), per dimension-vs-fixed-mechanic (#173 Fork B).

One block, shape dimension — not three blocks. shape: menubar | menu | context (default menu), with orientation derived. Menubar, popup menu, and context menu share the entire item/role/keyboard machinery and differ only in summoning (always-visible / trigger-click / pointer + Shift+F10) and orientation: a menubar adds tabindex=0 on its first item; a context menu binds the contextmenu event + Shift+F10. Matches Carbon/Ant and the platform's shared role machinery, rather than tripling the surface Radix-style (#173 Fork C).

Checkable items reuse Selection under a checkable trait. menuitemcheckbox / menuitemradio are genuine aria-checked selection state, not invocation, so they compose the Selection intent (model=single → radio group, model=multiple → independent checkboxes) under a checkable trait. A plain menuitem carries NO Selection (pure command). Matches the Fluent/Carbon/Radix consensus and avoids duplicating Selection's single-vs-multiple machinery (#173 Fork D).

Submenus compose Disclosure + Anchor — not the tree-select hierarchy intent. A submenu is a parent menuitem with aria-haspopup=menu + aria-expanded (Disclosure) opening its own Anchored menu surface, with the cascade owned by the menu keyboard model (Right opens / Left closes). The tree-select hierarchy intent is rejected: it owns flatten-to-visible-rows + aria-level/setsize/posinset + cross-level Up/Down traversal, none of which a cascading popup has — a submenu is a fresh anchored surface, a different paradigm (#173 Fork E).

The Menu block owns separator and group directly. role=separator (a non-focusable divider) and role=group (a labelled section) are first-class APG menu roles every library ships; the block owns them rather than deferring. The labelled-group case may lean on Selection's existing grouping dimension for the heading / aria-labelledby wiring; otherwise there is no cross-cutting reuse (#173 Fork F).

Claims the menu-button trigger; the droplist 'Menu button' roster entry is demoted to trigger-only. A true menu button (a button with aria-haspopup=menu / aria-expanded) opens a role=menu of commands — it belongs to this block, not the droplist family (a value-selector substrate). #173 claims the trigger; the droplist roster entry becomes 'the trigger shape only; surface owned by the Menu block' (the demotion is the follow-on #301) (#173 Fork G).

name: Notification
summary: The runnable surface that realizes the Feedback Intent — a transient, non-blocking message that appears, is accessibly announced, persists for a bounded time (or until dismissed), and is removed. It owns the general notification contract: a top-layer region host plus an injectable controller that app code calls imperatively to enqueue messages, with queueing/stacking, severity-mapped announcement, auto-timeout (paused on hover/focus, forced indefinite when an action is present), and manual dismissal. Presentation styles (toast, snackbar, banner-position, …) are the implementation's choice, not part of the contract — the block defines behaviour, the implementation picks the look. Native-first: the region mounts to the top layer via the Popover API where supported, falling back to a Web Portals mount. Composes the Message Intent (shared severity/tone vocabulary), the Live-Region Status Intent (announcement transport), the Surface Intent (card elevation/material), and the Motion Intent (enter/exit). Deliberately distinct from the OS-level Web Notifications API (system notifications, permission-gated — Web Permissions #009), from background-task-surface (#135, a persistent long-running-task surface), and from inline Message banners (structural, in-flow).
designDecisions: Named for the concept, not the presentation — `notification`, never `toast`. A toast is ONE presentation of a notification (a small transient card in a viewport corner); snackbars, banner-position notices, and status pills are others. The standard captures the general UX particularities every notification shares — temporary, non-blocking, dismissable, queued, severity-weighted, accessibly announced — and leaves the visual presentation to the implementation (Frontier UI). Baking 'toast' into the block name or contract would over-fit the standard to one look and is rejected (bias toward the most general, presentation-agnostic contract).

In-page UI, not the OS — distinct from the Web Notifications API. This block is in-document notification UI: a top-layer region inside the page. The browser's Notifications API (system/desktop notifications, permission-gated, surfacing outside the tab) shares the word but not the surface, the lifecycle, or the permission model — that is a separate capability owned by Web Permissions (#009). The two are cross-referenced and never conflated; choosing between them is the app's call (in-page reassurance vs. out-of-tab alert).

Component region + injectable controller — declarative placement, imperative trigger. The block ships a notification-region Component (the top-layer host, placed declaratively once per app) plus a controller resolved from the injector chain that arbitrary app logic calls to enqueue/dismiss (`show(message)` / `dismiss(id)`). Triggering is imperative because a notification originates from app events (a save succeeded, a request failed), not from a specific DOM trigger the way a tooltip or menu does. The standard defines the controller interface and the region element + its observable events; the concrete controller implementation (timing, animation, batching) is Frontier UI's, not the standard's.

Native-first top-layer surface — Popover API, then Web Portals. The region mounts to the platform top layer via the Popover API (popover="manual", so it is not light-dismissed by an unrelated click) where Baseline-supported, falling back to a Web Portals top-layer mount only where it is not. It is never a hand-rolled position:fixed + z-index stack — stacking-context bugs are exactly what the top layer exists to remove.

Announcement is severity-mapped and delegated to Live-Region Status. The region is a live region; politeness is derived from the Feedback severity, not hand-set per call: info/success → role="status" (polite, aria-live=polite), warning/error → role="alert" (assertive). The announcement transport (the live region, the politeness, the re-announce semantics) is the Live-Region Status Intent — this block composes it rather than re-implementing aria-live wiring. A notification's announcement is decoupled from its visual persistence: it is announced once when shown, even though it lingers on screen.

The transient contract: queue, timeout, dismissal — owned here as a contract, timed in the impl. stackLimit governs concurrency (1 = replace the current message, >1 = a FIFO stack up to the limit, surplus queued). duration drives an auto-timeout (short / long / indefinite) that is PAUSED while the pointer is over the region or focus is within it (so a reader is never raced by a timer — WCAG 2.2.1), and is FORCED to indefinite whenever an action is present (a user must never lose an actionable notification — e.g. an Undo — to an expiring timer; this is mandated by the Feedback Intent). Every notification is also manually dismissable (a close affordance) and Escape-dismissable. The block owns these as a queue-policy contract; the concrete timing constants and easing live in the implementation.

Transient and out-of-flow — distinct from background-task-surface and from inline Message. background-task-surface (#135) is a PERSISTENT surface that hosts long-running work after it goes async and outlives the originating view; a notification is TRANSIENT and fire-and-forget. The Message Intent is STRUCTURAL, in-flow messaging rendered in the document layout; a notification is an out-of-flow overlay in the top layer. The three are cross-referenced and kept separate (bias toward separation) — a forgivable failure may emit a notification, a long retry escalates to background-task-surface, and a persistent form-level error is an inline Message; conflating them loses the placement and lifecycle distinctions that make each correct.

name: Breadcrumb
summary: A structured trail showing the user's location within a site or data hierarchy, as an ordered list of links from root to current. Native-first: it IS a <nav aria-label="Breadcrumb"> wrapping an ordered list of <a> elements, with the current page a plain non-link node carrying aria-current="page" — the block adds only what the bare markup cannot express alone: overflow collapse when the trail is too wide for its container, and a separator that is presentational (CSS ::before / aria-hidden), never an announced character. It composes the Navigation Intent (it is a navigation landmark) and the Typography Intent (the responsible-truncation paradigm for a long crumb label). Deliberately distinct from nav-list (APG Disclosure Navigation menus), from tabs (panel switching), and from a stepper (a linear process, not a location).
designDecisions:
A breadcrumb IS a nav landmark wrapping an ordered list — enhance the native markup, don't replace it. The accessible baseline is
  1. …
    2. …
with the last item a non-link carrying aria-current="page". This is zero-JS and fully accessible on its own, so the block defaults to it and adds code only for the two things the markup can't do alone: overflow collapse and responsible label truncation. Reinventing it as a custom widget would discard the free landmark + list semantics for nothing.

The separator is presentational, never content. The visual divider between crumbs (›, /, →) is rendered via CSS (li ::before) or marked aria-hidden, so assistive tech reads 'Home, Products, Widgets' — the list structure conveys order — not 'Home slash Products slash Widgets'. A separator baked into the DOM as text is an accessibility anti-pattern this block forbids.

Overflow is collapse, not wrap or scroll. When the trail exceeds its container the middle is collapsed behind a single disclosure (an ellipsis crumb that reveals the hidden ancestors), keeping the first (root) and last (current) crumbs visible — the two a user needs most. The per-crumb label truncation is the Typography Intent's responsible-truncation paradigm (shared with tabs, chips, table cells), not a breadcrumb-specific mechanism.

Location, not navigation menu and not process — distinct from nav-list, tabs, and stepper. A breadcrumb answers 'where am I in the hierarchy?' (a trail of ancestors), which is a different question from nav-list's 'what can I navigate to?' (a disclosure menu), tabs' 'which panel is shown?' (sibling panels), and a stepper's 'how far through this process?' (linear steps). It composes the Navigation Intent like nav-list but is its own pattern with its own ARIA (aria-current="page", ordered list), cross-referenced rather than folded into nav-list.

name: Dialog
summary: The abstract substrate every dialog-based surface conforms to — a top-layer surface that interrupts or overlays the page, owning the shared modal-surface machinery: focus containment, escape/outside/backdrop dismissal, scroll-lock, modal-vs-non-modal mode, and the native-first top-layer mount. Like droplist, it is not a shippable widget — it is the family substrate. Its concrete members are each their own block, distinct configurations of the Modal Intent's placement over this one substrate: a centered modal (placement=center), an edge Drawer (placement=start/end + slide, #360), and a bottom Sheet (placement=bottom). Native-first by construction: built on the native <dialog> element (showModal for the modal focus-trap, inert background, and ::backdrop; the platform top layer) and the Popover API for the non-modal case — never a position:fixed + z-index re-implementation. It realizes the Modal Intent and composes Focus Containment (the trap), Surface (elevation/material), and Motion (enter/exit).
designDecisions: Family substrate, not a shippable widget — droplist applied to dialogs. A dialog's hard parts (focus containment, dismissal, backdrop, scroll-lock, top-layer mounting, modal/non-modal mode) are the shared modal-surface machinery EVERY dialog-based surface needs, so dialog declares the substrate once and concrete members compose it — exactly as droplist is the abstract substrate for Dropdown/Autocomplete/Tree-select. Keeping the family thin and the machinery in one place is why a drawer need not re-implement any of it.

Family vs. members: concrete members are each their own block, not configurations of one widget. A centered Modal (placement=center), an edge Drawer (placement=start/end + slide, #360), and a bottom Sheet (placement=bottom) are distinct members — each a fixed configuration of the Modal Intent's placement over this substrate — rather than one dialog widget toggled by a flag. This mirrors droplist's family/members ruling (Dropdown, Multi-dropdown, Autocomplete are separate blocks) and the menu block's distinct-pattern stance, and keeps each member's spec honest about what it fixes.

Native-first: built on <dialog> + the top layer, never re-implemented. The native <dialog> element with showModal() already provides the modal focus-trap, the inert background, the ::backdrop, Escape-to-cancel, and a real top-layer paint — so the substrate defaults to it and enhances only what the platform doesn't give (placement other than center, scroll-lock nuance, coordinated enter/exit motion, swipe). The non-modal mode uses dialog.show() or the Popover API. A position:fixed + z-index stack is rejected — it is exactly the stacking-context class of bug the top layer removes.

Modal vs non-modal is the Modal Intent's `mode` dimension, owned here so members inherit it. mode=modal → showModal() (focus-trapping, background inert, backdrop); mode=non-modal → show()/popover (focus stays free, page interactive). The substrate owns this branch — and the Modal Intent's `dismissal`, `backdrop`, and `scrolling` dimensions are realized here once — so a concrete member only fixes `placement` (+ motion). This is the concrete reason a drawer composes, not re-implements, the dialog.

implementsIntent stays 'modal' (the contract it most directly realizes) so intent→block reverse-lookup is unchanged; composesIntents lists the full set (Focus Containment, Surface, Motion), surfaced on the block page and each composed intent's page — the same convention droplist uses with 'anchor'.

name: Drawer
summary: The edge-anchored, sliding concrete member of the dialog family — a panel that enters from a viewport edge (a navigation drawer, a filter panel, a detail pane). It does NOT re-implement the modal-surface machinery: it extends the abstract dialog block (focus containment, escape/outside/backdrop dismissal, scroll-lock, modal/non-modal mode, top-layer mount are all inherited) and fixes the configuration to an edge — the Modal Intent's placement=start|end (RTL-aware), with a slide-in/out motion and swipe-to-dismiss added on top of the inherited dismissal. Relationship to the dialog family mirrors dropdown to droplist: a concrete member, not a standalone widget and not a flag on one widget. Its siblings are the centered modal (placement=center) and the bottom sheet (placement=bottom).
designDecisions: Extends the dialog substrate — inherits the machinery, adds nothing it can avoid. Focus containment, escape/outside/backdrop dismissal, scroll-lock, modal/non-modal mode, and the native-first top-layer mount all come from the abstract dialog block; the drawer re-implements none of them. This is the whole point of the family substrate (droplist:dropdown :: dialog:drawer) — the drawer's spec is small because it only describes what it fixes, not how a dialog works.

A drawer IS Modal placement=start|end, RTL-aware. The edge is the Modal Intent's placement dimension fixed to a viewport inline edge (start/end, which mirror under RTL), distinguishing it from the centered modal (center) and bottom sheet (bottom) siblings. The drawer adds a slide-in/out motion (the Motion Intent) along that edge — the visual signature of a drawer over a fade-in modal.

Swipe-to-dismiss layered on inherited dismissal, never replacing it. Because a drawer is touch-prominent (mobile navigation, filter panels), it adds an edge-swipe gesture to dismiss, on top of — not instead of — the dialog substrate's escape/outside/backdrop dismissal. The gesture is an enhancement; the keyboard/pointer dismissal it inherits remains the accessible baseline.

Member, not widget-with-a-flag — distinct block from modal and sheet. Drawer, centered modal, and bottom sheet are separate concrete members (distinct placements over the shared substrate), following the dialog family's family-vs-members ruling and droplist's precedent (Dropdown, Multi-dropdown, Autocomplete are separate blocks). A `side` is the drawer's own small axis (which edge); the cross-member differences live in the placement that defines each member.

name: Carousel
summary: A set of equivalent slides presented one (or a few) at a time, advanced by controls, pagination markers, swipe, or optional autoplay. Native-first by construction: the substrate is a CSS scroll-snap container (scroll-snap-type + scroll-snap-align gives paging, momentum, touch, and keyboard scrolling for free), and where supported the emerging CSS carousel pseudo-elements (::scroll-button() for prev/next, ::scroll-marker / scroll-marker-group for the dots) provide native controls — the block leans on them rather than re-implementing scroll logic in JS. It adds the WAI-ARIA APG carousel accessibility (rotation announced via a live region; autoplay opt-in, paused on hover/focus, honoring prefers-reduced-motion, with a required pause control) and composes the Motion, Live-Region Status, and Navigation intents. Distinct from tabs (labelled panels, not equivalent slides) and from paginated data (the Collection Operations intent).
designDecisions: Native-first: CSS scroll-snap is the baseline; the CSS carousel pseudo-elements are the frontier. A scroll-snap container (scroll-snap-type: x mandatory + scroll-snap-align on each slide) already gives paging, momentum, touch, and arrow-key scrolling with zero JS, so the block defaults to it. Where the emerging CSS Overflow carousel features ship (::scroll-button() for prev/next, ::scroll-marker + scroll-marker-group for pagination dots — the Open UI / CSS WG work), the block uses them as native controls instead of JS handlers. Custom JS paging is the fallback for browsers without them, never the baseline.

WAI-ARIA APG carousel accessibility is mandatory, not optional. The rotating region announces the current slide via a live region; the carousel exposes prev/next and a slide-picker (markers); per the APG, slides are a group (or tabpanel-style) with accessible names. The block bakes these in — a carousel that is just a scroll-snap strip with no announced rotation and no controls is not conformant and not what this block ships.

Autoplay is opt-in and accessibility-gated. Autoplay is OFF by default; when enabled it must pause on hover and on keyboard focus within the carousel, must NOT auto-advance under prefers-reduced-motion, and must expose a visible pause/play control (WCAG 2.2.2 Pause, Stop, Hide). These are fixed obligations of the autoplay configuration, not author choices — an unpausable auto-rotating carousel is an accessibility failure the block forbids.

Equivalent slides, not labelled panels and not paginated data — distinct from tabs and Collection Operations. A carousel rotates through peer content the user can browse (promos, images, cards); tabs switch between distinctly-labelled panels via a tablist; Collection Operations pages/filters/sorts a data set. A carousel composes Navigation for its prev/next + markers but is its own pattern, cross-referenced rather than merged.

name: Checkbox
summary: Native-first over `<input type="checkbox">`: a single boolean control, a checkbox group (multi-select), and the `indeterminate` (mixed) tri-state. Realizes the `input` boolean control and composes `selection` (model: multiple) for groups; the indeterminate state maps to `aria-checked="mixed"` on a group parent. Zero JS for the single/standalone case (the native element IS the block); a thin behavior only coordinates a group's mixed-state parent. Sliced from #468 (form-control inventory).
designDecisions: Native-first: the standalone checkbox is the bare `<input type="checkbox">` with a `<label>` — no JS, no wrapper element. The block adds value only for the GROUP case (a set of checkboxes with a select-all/mixed parent) and the programmatic `indeterminate` property the platform exposes but never sets itself.

Indeterminate is the platform tri-state, not a coined one: `el.indeterminate = true` renders the mixed glyph and the group parent exposes `aria-checked="mixed"` (WAI-ARIA tri-state checkbox). It is a display/ARIA state, never a submitted value — a checkbox still submits checked/unchecked.

Distinct from toggle/switch (#473): a checkbox is a deferred form value (committed on submit, reversible before), a switch is an immediate on/off action. Same `<input type=checkbox>` substrate, different semantics and role — kept as separate blocks, not one with a mode flag.

name: Toggle / Switch
summary: Native-first over `<input type="checkbox">` with `role="switch"`: a boolean on/off control whose change takes effect IMMEDIATELY, distinct from a checkbox's deferred-until-submit form value. Realizes the `input` boolean control. `aria-checked` true/false reflects on/off; no mixed/indeterminate state (a switch is strictly binary). Zero JS — the native element plus `role="switch"` and styling IS the block. Sliced from #468 (form-control inventory).
designDecisions: Native-first: a switch is the platform `<input type="checkbox" role="switch">` (or a `<button aria-pressed>` for a pure action) — no custom element. The block standardizes the role + label + immediate-effect contract, not a new widget.

Immediate vs. deferred is THE distinction from checkbox (#472): a switch applies its state on toggle (e.g. enable a setting now); a checkbox is a form value committed on submit. Same `<input type=checkbox>` substrate, different role and timing — separate blocks, never a checkbox `mode` flag.

Strictly binary: a switch has no `indeterminate`/`mixed` state (unlike the checkbox). `aria-checked` is true/false only; the WAI-ARIA APG Switch pattern with `<kbd>Space</kbd>` toggle and an on/off accessible name.

name: Radio Group
summary: Native-first over a `<input type="radio">` group sharing a `name`: single-select among mutually-exclusive choices. Realizes the `selection` intent (model: single) and composes `focus-delegation` (roving — arrow keys move AND select within the group, the group is a single Tab stop). Native exclusivity comes free from the shared `name`; a `<fieldset>` + `<legend>` names the group. Zero JS for the standard case. Sliced from #468 (form-control inventory).
designDecisions: Native-first: a radio group is N `<input type="radio">` sharing a `name`, wrapped in `<fieldset>`/`<legend>` — mutual exclusivity and the single submitted value are the platform's, no JS. The block standardizes the grouping + labelling contract.

Roving focus is the platform's for native radios: arrow keys move focus AND change selection, and the group is ONE Tab stop (the checked radio, or the first when none). This is exactly the focus-delegation `roving` value — the block composes that intent rather than redefining traversal.

Single-select, distinct from a checkbox group (#472): radios are mutually exclusive (pick one), a checkbox group is multi-select (pick any). Maps to selection `model: single` vs `multiple`. A radio group has no indeterminate state but can start with none selected.

name: Input Family
summary: Native-first over the text-ish `<input>` family (text / number / currency / phone / masked): the formatting, masking, and typing affordances the bare element can't express, while keeping the native input as the substrate. Realizes the `input` intent. The platform `type` (`text`/`number`/`tel`/`email`/`url`) carries semantics, keyboard, and validation for free; the block adds a display-format layer (Intl) and an optional input mask over a raw value, never replacing the element. Zero JS for a plain typed input; the format/mask behavior is opt-in. Sliced from #468 (form-control inventory).
designDecisions: Native-first: the substrate is always a native `<input>` with the most specific platform `type` (`number` for numerics, `tel` for phone, `email`/`url` where they fit) — the type gives the mobile keyboard, the constraint-validation hooks, and the submitted value for free. The block never swaps the element for a custom widget; it layers formatting/masking on top.

Display value vs. raw value are kept separate (the data-table formatter rule, #368): currency/number formatting is presentational via `Intl.NumberFormat` (and locale-aware grouping/decimals), while the form-submitted/queried value stays the unformatted raw — so `1234.5` submits as `1234.5` even when shown as `$1,234.50`. A `type=number` input can't show grouped currency, so a currency field is a `type=text inputmode=decimal` with the Intl layer, raw value mirrored to a hidden/native value.

Masking is the one affordance the platform lacks: a phone/SSN/date mask constrains and segments typing (`(555) 123-4567`) — the block standardizes a declarative mask pattern over the raw digits, never `innerHTML`, and degrades to a plain typed input when JS is off. Masks are opt-in, distinct from constraint validation (`pattern`/`maxlength`), which stays native.

name: Form
summary: The form-level state machine and lifecycle actions, sitting ABOVE the field-level Validation Intent / Web Validation (which own per-field rules and validity). Web Validation answers 'is this field valid?'; the Form block answers 'what is the state of the whole form, and how do I reset / submit / initialize it?' Grounded in the native `<form>` element, `FormData`, the Constraint Validation API and `formAssociated` custom elements — it adds only the aggregate state and lifecycle actions the platform doesn't expose, and composes existing intents for everything else. Candidate standard (no implementation yet); composition manifest like Droplist.
designDecisions: Sits above the field layer, never duplicating it: per-field rules and validity belong to the Validation Intent / Web Validation project. The Form block owns only the form-LEVEL aggregate (valid/invalid, touched/untouched, focused/unfocused, dirty/pristine across fields) and the lifecycle actions (submit, reset, clear, initialize). Composition over redefinition, the Droplist rule.

Native-first substrate: the block augments a real `<form>` rather than replacing it. `FormData` reads the current values, the Constraint Validation API supplies per-field validity, and `formAssociated` custom elements (via `ElementInternals` form-lifecycle callbacks: formResetCallback, formStateRestoreCallback) let custom fields participate in the native form. The block adds aggregate state + actions the platform leaves to userland; it never re-implements field validity or submission the platform already does.

Form validity merges from field validity through the swappable ValidityMergeRegistry (#212 / #004 OP-1), NOT a merge rule baked into the Form block. The standard consumes the `MergedValidity` hand-off shape; HOW field results reduce to form validity (native-first default: source-reduction over named sources with declared precedence and a `pending{version}` token) is the registry's swappable concern. This closes the item's open 'last-writer vs strictest' question by deferring it to the already-ratified strategy plane.

Dirty / pristine is current-vs-initial, and `initialize(initialValues?)` re-baselines it: `initialValues` (the seed), `defaultValues` (reset target), and current `values` are kept distinct. dirty = current ≠ initial; reset → defaults; clear → empty; initialize → re-seed AND mark pristine (the new baseline). Three value sets, never one overloaded field — mirrors the three-distinct-dates discipline elsewhere.

Composes, not re-specifies, three intents for the rest: Validation Intent (field validity merges up to form validity), Exit Guard (the unsaved-changes navigation guard #129, armed from `dirty`), and Feedback/Reliability (submit lifecycle: in-flight, success, error). Each behavior defers to the owning intent, keeping the Form spec thin.
designRationale: Surfaced as a gap: there is no block answering the whole-form state/lifecycle question, only field-level validity. Built fresh on the native form + validation primitives (the legacy plateau repo is explicitly NOT a model and was not consulted). Authored as a candidate (draft) composition manifest; a reference implementation is a follow-on once the contract is reviewed.

name: Reaction
summary: Affix-a-symbol-to-content interaction: each reaction is an `aria-pressed` toggle backed by the Reaction Intent. Composes the expressive-symbol glyph (never re-renders it) and adopts Atlassian ReactionSummary as the contract baseline (count · users[] · reacted · optimisticallyUpdated). The only first-class precedent (Atlassian @atlaskit/reactions) is exactly this block+intent shape. Per #370 Fork 5.
