designDecisions: Block + intent (#370 Fork 5): reaction IS runnable behavior (toggle-your-own, aggregate, optimistic update), so the standard owns the mechanics once instead of leaving every consumer to re-implement them — an intent alone would under-specify the very thing that makes a reaction a reaction.

Contract baseline = Atlassian `ReactionSummary`: `count` (aggregate) · `users[]` (who-reacted roster, display-limited) · `reacted` (toggle-your-own) · `optimisticallyUpdated`. Classified a DATA CONTRACT, NOT a protocol — no swappable-vendor/engine-interop story, so a protocol would be lock-in for no interop gain.

Multi-user sync is COMPOSED, never baked in: the "others see your reaction" concern is a separate real-time protocol (webrealtime); the reaction contract stays sync-agnostic. Count changes announce through `live-region-status`.

Accessibility (load-bearing): each reaction is an `aria-pressed` toggle with a COMPOSED accessible name (emoji name + count + your-reacted-state — "thumbs up, 5 reactions, you reacted, activate to remove"), not a bare glyph. Announce YOUR LOCAL toggle only — never every remote tick. The roster is keyboard-reachable.

Reaction set is an open, config-driven named-set registry (#370 Fork 6): default-less core, convergent `sentiment` default, `allowAllEmojis` escape to the full picker. Composes the expressive-symbol render strategy for the glyphs.
vs #354 status badge: a reaction count chip resembles a `surface`-tokened badge but means "N people reacted," not "this state" — they compose, they do not merge.

name: Picker Surface
summary: The one general browse↔search picker surface per #370 Fork 4 (compose, don't reinvent): a `popover` container + the search `input` intent + a 2-D category grid, reusing the droplist/autocomplete substrate rather than a bespoke emoji-picker block. Owns exactly one thing — the load-bearing APG Grid (2-D arrow browse) ↔ Combobox/listbox (search) focus hand-off — and defers everything else to the intent that owns it. One reusable surface instantiated by many specific picker contexts (full-page filtered collection, in-editor tooltip picker, reaction quick-picker), each its own later story.
designDecisions: Compose, don't reinvent (#370 Fork 4, ratified): the symbol picker is a composition surface, not a new emoji-picker block — popover container (anchor) + search field (input) + a category grid, reusing the droplist/autocomplete substrate that already owns positioning, focus, selection, async, and truncation. It is NOT a droplist family member: droplist members are 1-D value-selector listboxes (Dropdown/Multi/Autocomplete/Tree-select), whereas the picker is a 2-D browse grid that hands off to a 1-D search listbox — a different shape, not a trait selection over the same substrate.

The load-bearing transform — Grid↔Combobox focus hand-off: BROWSE renders a `role=grid` of categories navigated 2-D (focus-delegation strategy=roving|virtual, orientation=both — Up/Down/Left/Right across ragged emoji rows, Home/End per row). SEARCH renders a `role=listbox` navigated 1-D (focus-delegation strategy=virtual, orientation=vertical, controller=<search-input>, aria-activedescendant on the input). DOM focus stays on the search input throughout; the hand-off is query-driven — empty query → the grid is the active surface, a non-empty query → the listbox is the active surface, Escape/clear returns to the grid. This swap (which surface owns the active descendant + the grid↔listbox role/dimensionality change) is the single piece of logic this block owns.

Promotion-trigger determination (the pinned condition from #370 Fork 4): RESOLVED — promote to a thin block. The trigger was 'promote only if the Grid↔Combobox hand-off cannot be expressed as configuration over the existing droplist/autocomplete.' It cannot: `autocomplete` is the 1-D combobox half only and has no browse-grid mode, so the picker is not a configuration of autocomplete but a composition of autocomplete's search half with a 2-D grid plus a mode switch. Each half is individually a focus-delegation config (so the block defers focus mechanics and stays THIN — it re-specifies none of them), but coordinating the query-driven swap between two focus-delegation profiles on two surfaces is bespoke focus-coordination logic no intent owns. Reinforcing evidence: focus-delegation's own open question flags that '2-D grid mechanics (row/column wrap, cell vs. widget focus, ragged rows) need a deeper contract than orientation:both currently implies' — the ragged final emoji row is exactly that gap. The block therefore owns ONLY the hand-off; everything else is deferred.

One general surface, many specific contexts: what this block ratifies is the reusable browse↔search composition, NOT a single picker widget. It is instantiated by several specific picker contexts — each its own later story because trigger, density, and container differ: a full-page filtered collection (the Web Docs 'shop' consumer, #398), an in-editor inline/tooltip picker (a :-trigger popover anchored to the caret — blocked on the not-yet-existing rich-text/contenteditable + mention-trigger standards), and the reaction quick-picker (the small popover the reaction bar opens for allowAllEmojis, #370 Fork 6). Two consumers already justify compose over a bespoke block: a runtime end-user picking a symbol to react/insert, and an authoring-time dev/designer shopping the icon+emoji+sticker catalog inside Web Docs (the see/shop paradigm of #236/#283).

Accessibility is composed, not baked: result-count announcements on filter settle ('7 results available' / 'No results found') and the active option on each move ('grinning face, 2 of 84') go through live-region-status — one shared announcer, two callers (filter counts + active-descendant moves), exactly as autocomplete already wires it. Large emoji sets are virtualized via windowed-collection; the rendered glyphs are deferred to the expressive-symbol intent (the picker shops them, never re-renders them).

name: Inline Trigger
summary: The runnable trigger-detection engine ratified in #591 (Fork 1-C): an editable region that detects a boundary-anchored trigger char (@/:/#//), slices the query, anchors a popover to the caret (virtual element via the anchor intent), and routes results through the picker surface / autocomplete listbox. Detection-only per Fork 2-A — emits the resolved selection {key, query, range, item} and lets the consumer commit, owning no document model, so it works uniformly in <input>/<textarea>/contenteditable and composes the rich-text standard (#590) for token insertion without being blocked on it. Ships reference text + token insertion adapters but owns neither commit. Forced invariants: APG editable-combobox virtual focus (DOM focus stays in the text, aria-activedescendant drives the listbox), IME-composition suspension, not-a-protocol.
designDecisions: Detection-only / insertion-agnostic (Fork 2-A, the divergent ruling): the block emits the resolved selection {key, query, range, item} and stops; the consumer commits. This is the GitHub <text-expander> model (text-expander-value hands you the replacement), NOT the ProseMirror Suggestion.command model (which couples insertion into the editor doc). Owning no document model is exactly what lets one engine run unchanged in <input>, <textarea>, and contenteditable, and compose the rich-text standard (#590) for token insertion WITHOUT being blocked on it. The items-source (results provider) and the committer are consumer-supplied behaviors, never hardcoded.

New block + intent, not a mode of autocomplete (Fork 1-C): the trigger-detection engine (boundary-anchored char match -> query slice -> caret anchor -> result routing) is runnable behavior every prior-art system ships as code (ProseMirror/Tiptap Suggestion, GitHub <text-expander>, Lexical useBasicTypeaheadTriggerMatch); folding it into the autocomplete block (A) or shipping intent-only (B) both under-specify the engine. The declarative dimensions (trigger set, boundary policy, query policy, insertion mode) are the paired intent. Not a protocol — a deterministic transform with no swappable-vendor interop story.

Caret anchoring is the one genuinely new mechanic, and it composes existing intents: the popover tethers to the caret — a virtual element, not a stable DOM node — composing the anchor intent (placement/collision/flip) over a caret rect. In contenteditable the DOM Range.getBoundingClientRect() gives the rect; in <input>/<textarea> a mirror-div caret-coordinate measurement is needed. Off-screen-near-edge bugs in the prior art (Lexical #3834) confirm collision handling is load-bearing, so the block defers it to anchor rather than re-implementing positioning.

Accessibility is the APG editable combobox, verbatim (forced invariant): DOM focus stays on the editable region (virtual focus); the listbox is navigated via aria-activedescendant with the active option scrolled into view by JS (browsers don't manage visibility for aria-activedescendant). The textbox carries aria-expanded/aria-controls/aria-autocomplete. A roving tabindex would steal focus from the text and is broken. Detection suspends during IME composition (composing a trigger char in a CJK IME must not fire the picker). Match counts announce through live-region-status. No dedicated 'mention' APG pattern exists — everyone reuses the combobox pattern; the native-first baseline degrades to a plain textbox with no inline suggestions.

Reference insertion adapters, owning neither commit: ships text (default — replaces the query range with a string, works in a plain <textarea>) and token (composes #590's atom/token insertion). Both are reference adapters the standard provides for the two insertion modes the intent exposes; the consumer selects or supplies one. This keeps #591 decoupled from #590 — composed, not blocked — exactly as the Fork 2-A ruling requires.

name: Data Transfer Zone
summary: A drop-zone runtime that collapses the three native sources of a DataTransfer payload — drag-and-drop, clipboard paste, and <input type=file> — into one normalized `receive` event under a declared `accepts` contract (type/size), dispatching `reject` for anything that fails, with a keyboard/a11y file-picker affordance, the copy-vs-move flow as the drag dropEffect, and a copy-out (`emit`) half.

dataTransfer.pattern: The native DataTransfer object shared by drag events and the clipboard
dataTransfer.adoption: normalizeDataTransfer() reads DataTransferItemList (file vs string kinds) with a files+getData fallback, so drop and paste collapse to one shape.

dragAndDrop.pattern: HTML Drag and Drop API (dragover.preventDefault to become a drop target; dropEffect)
dragAndDrop.adoption: The zone mirrors its configured copy/move effect onto dataTransfer.dropEffect during dragover.

clipboardEvent.adoption: Paste funnels through the same normalizer; copy writes the queued emit payload via setData.

fileInput.pattern: <input type=file multiple> as the keyboard-accessible source
fileInput.adoption: An always-present hidden file input behind a real <button> gives a keyboard/a11y path; drag and paste are pointer/clipboard sugar on top.

receive.description: An accepted, normalized payload entered the zone (from drop, paste, or the file input)

reject.description: An incoming payload failed the declared accepts contract (kind, type, or size)

emit.description: The zone wrote a typed payload onto an outgoing drag or copy (the emit flow)

singleFunnel.description: Drop, paste, and file-input all converge on one private #ingest()
singleFunnel.rationale: The intent is "one payload event regardless of source" — a single funnel guarantees the accepts contract and the receive/reject decision are applied identically to all three.

nativeFirst.description: No synthetic drag model or library — the platform DataTransfer/DragEvent/ClipboardEvent APIs only
nativeFirst.rationale: Native-first default: the runtime is the web platform; libraries would be opt-in enhancements, not the baseline.

keyboardPathIsPrimary.description: The hidden <input type=file> behind a <button> is always present, not a fallback
keyboardPathIsPrimary.rationale: Drag-and-drop is inaccessible on its own; the keyboard-triggered file picker is the mandatory path, with drag/paste layered as pointer sugar.

pureLogicSplit.description: Normalization + acceptance live in DOM-free normalize.ts; the element only wires events
pureLogicSplit.rationale: Keeps the gnarly MIME/kind/size logic unit-testable without a DOM and reusable outside the element.

permissiveDefault.description: A zone with no constraints defaults to `any` acceptance
permissiveDefault.rationale: Most-flexible default — the restriction is the author opt-in (declare accepts/accept-types/max-size).

name: Draft Persistence
summary: Durable save-and-resume of an entity's working state (the #011 storage facet of webstates): autosave-on-change snapshots restored to the exact field state across sessions, behind a pluggable CustomStorageStrategy (IndexedDB native-first, localStorage degradation, in-memory test fallback), plus the last-writer-wins co-edit primitive (per-editor token + savedAt compare + an "also editing" presence signal over BroadcastChannel/storage).

indexedDB.pattern: IndexedDB as the durable structured-record store
indexedDB.adoption: IndexedDbStorageStrategy is the native-first default behind the CustomStorageStrategy seam (#011 Fork 1A).

storagePersist.adoption: requestPersistent() best-effort marks the drafts durable so the OS will not evict them under pressure.

broadcastChannel.adoption: CoEditCoordinator broadcasts hello/saved/bye; falls back to localStorage + the storage event where BroadcastChannel is absent.

webStorage.adoption: LocalStorageStrategy is the degradation path when IndexedDB is unavailable; prefixed keys keep it namespaced.

coedit-presence.description: A peer editor on the same entity was seen (joined/left) — the "X also editing" signal

coedit-conflict.description: A peer saved the same entity — compare savedAt and reconcile by last-writer-wins

storageFacetOfWebstates.description: Persistence is a thin storage facet of webstates, not a new top-level project
storageFacetOfWebstates.rationale: #011 Fork 1A: webstates already owns "the data the app holds"; durable persistence is that concern extended to disk. A CustomStorageStrategy seam, not a monolith.

nativeFirstWithDegradation.description: IndexedDB default → localStorage degradation → in-memory test fallback, one async contract
nativeFirstWithDegradation.rationale: Native-first default with deterministic degradation; the in-memory fallback keeps the controller testable and SSR-safe.

lastWriterWins.description: Co-edit conflict resolves by savedAt comparison, ties to remote
lastWriterWins.rationale: #011 Fork 3: conflict resolution is a change-tracking strategy gated to the concurrent-editor case; last-writer-wins is the simplest correct default, the merge policy left to the caller.

presenceNotMerge.description: CoEditCoordinator surfaces who-else-is-editing + a conflict signal; it does not merge state
presenceNotMerge.rationale: Detection and policy are separable — the coordinator signals; resolveLww (or a richer change-tracking strategy) decides.

pureLogicSplit.description: resolveLww and the storage strategies are DOM/IO-isolated from the controller
pureLogicSplit.rationale: Keeps the LWW decision and each store unit-testable without IndexedDB or multiple tabs.
